content.text: What Must Appear on a Dispensing Label?

content.body: The dispensing label is the primary source of medication information for the patient after they leave the pharmacy. An incomplete or unclear label can lead to medication errors, missed doses, or dangerous misuse. Each Caribbean island has specific legal requirements for what must appear on a dispensing label.

content.title: Mandatory Label Elements by Island

islands.name: Trinidad & Tobago
details: Pharmacy name, address, and telephone number
Storage instructions (especially important in tropical climate)
'Keep out of reach of children' warning

islands.name: Jamaica
details: Name and address of the dispensing pharmacy
Name of medication (generic name required by policy in public facilities)
'Keep out of reach of children' required on all labels
National Health Fund (NHF) number if applicable for subsidised medications

islands.name: Barbados
details: Name of medication (Barbados National Drug Formulary name preferred)
Directions for use in clear, plain English
Barbados Drug Service lot/batch tracking number (public sector)
'Keep out of reach of children' mandatory

content.description: While there is significant overlap, each jurisdiction has specific requirements. Always follow the requirements of the island where you are practising.

content.body: Jamaica's National Health Fund (NHF) programme strongly encourages generic prescribing and labelling. In Barbados, the National Drug Formulary also favours generic names. In Trinidad, both generic and brand names may appear. When in doubt, include both the generic and brand name on the label to avoid patient confusion, especially if a patient has been switched from a brand to a generic equivalent.
content.title: Generic vs Brand Name Labelling
Label Printing Demo: Common Pharmacy Software
content.description: Walkthrough of generating dispensing labels using common pharmacy management software used in Caribbean pharmacies, including configuration of mandatory fields and auxiliary warning labels.
